title: practical english usage - Part 125
content:
We   can put all after pronouns used as objects.

3  pronoun       + all Section13 Determiners:       Quantifiers grammar •  149   all, everybody/everyone and everything my whole life - all (of) my life Julie spent the whole summer  at home.

- Julie spent all (of) the summer  at home.

determiner + whole + noun till (of) + determiner + noun mean 'complete', 'every part of. The  word order is different.

All (of) (especially BrE) and whole can be used with singular noun phrases to 2 word    order all /ɔ:l/              whole /həol/ 1  pronunciation 150 all   and   whole SPY   TELLS   ALL).

English in dramatic contexts like newspaper headlines (e.g.

(e.g. Tell me all; All is lost; All are dead). This only happens regularly in modern In older English, all could be used alone to mean 'everybody' or 'everything' 3 older    English Note also That's all (= It's finished; There's no more).

All that happened was that he went to sleep.

This is all I've got. All I want is a place to sit down.

‘ nothing more' or 'the only thing(s)'.

This structure often has a rather negative meaning, expressing ideas like not She lost everything, (     She lost aU.) - She lost all (that) she owned.

Everything is yours, (     AU is yours.) not - All (that) I have is yours, (     AU-wkatl have...) structure all + relative clause (all that...). Compare: All (without a noun phrase) can mean 'everything', but usually only in the 2  all   and   everything not Everybody/Everyone stood up. (     AU stood up.) All the people stood up.

We   do not normally use all without a noun phrase to mean 'everybody*.

1  all   and   everybody/everyone 149 all,   everybody/everyone                    and   everything All of us can swim.  All (of) the guests have arrived.

subject (►   147).

Note that these meanings can also be expressed by using all (of) with the grammar »151    every (one) His books are great. Every one's worth reading.               -> We   can drop a noun and use every one alone, if the meaning is clear.

3 every      one     without      a  noun Every one of the children was crying.

His books are wonderful. I've read every one of them.

every one of+ determiner + plural noun (+ singular verb) every one of us/you/them (+ singular verb) these). The  pronoun or noun is plural, but a following verb is singular.

We use every one of before a pronoun or a determiner (for example the, my, 2 every      one     of not Every room is being used, (     Every room are.. .) not I see her every day. (     .. .-every days?) every + singular noun (+ singular verb) below). If the noun is a subject, its verb is also singular.

Every is a determiner. We   normally use it before a singular noun (but ►     151.5 1  every      + singular ɪʒl   every      (one)   __________________ was left alive in these tribes.) Whole Indian tribes were killed off. (= Complete tribes were killed off; nobody tribe suffered ...) All Indian tribes suffered from white settlement in America. (= Every Indian means 'complete*, 'entire*. Compare: With plural nouns, all and whole have different meanings. All is like every; whole 6 plural     nouns I've read the whole of / all of it.

I've just read the whole of / all of 'War and Peace'.

The whole of / All of Venice was under water, (     Whole-Veniee...) whole. All (of) is also possible.

Before proper nouns (names) and pronouns we  always use the whole of, not the whole of my life Julie spent the whole of the summer  at home.

Instead of whole, the whole of is also possible, especially in British English.

5 the    whole       of  (mainly     BrE) not I've drunk all (of) the milk, (     ... the whole mUte.) With most uncountable nouns we  prefer all (of).

4 uncountable         nouns not She's eaten a whole loaf, (     ... all a loaf) All is not generally used before indefinite articles.

3  indefinite       reference Section13 Determiners:       Quantifiers grammar «151    every (one) For more information about everybody/everyone, ►    180.

For the difference between every and each, »-154.       For every and all, ►    152.   For every and any, ►     1 62.
